title: Chitin Salvage
desc: Shed chitin is recycled into armor and tools. Waste nothing.

steps.text:
Danians shed chitin naturally — old plates loosen, crack, and fall away as new growth replaces them. In the wild, shed chitin would decompose. In the hive, it's a resource. Chitin is collected, processed, and recycled into armor plates, tool handles, construction materials, and a dozen other products.

The salvage station at the barracks handles the military-grade collection — shed chitin from Mandiblors, whose plates are thicker and harder than civilian chitin due to their combat conditioning.

The station master sorts today's intake. "Grade the plates. Category one: thick, uncracked, usable for armor. Category two: thin or cracked, suitable for tools. Category three: fragments, goes to the grinder for composite material."

She drops a piece into your hands. It's surprisingly light — hard as ceramic, amber-colored, curved to fit a Mandiblor's shoulder.

options.label: Grade the intake — sort by quality
Work the grinder — process category three

steps.text:
Grading requires a trained eye. You hold each plate to the light, checking for internal fractures — hairline cracks that weaken the structure. You flex each plate gently — good chitin flexes without breaking, like a stiff leather. Bad chitin snaps.

The intake is sixty-eight pieces. You sort them methodically. Category one: fourteen plates, thick and flawless. Category two: thirty-one plates, usable but imperfect. Category three: twenty-three fragments, too small or too damaged for anything but grinding.

Among the category one plates, you find a standout — a thorax plate, almost perfectly preserved, thick as a coin and hard enough to ring when you tap it. The station master whistles.

"That's officer-grade chitin. The conditioning regimen produces denser plates in senior Mandiblors." She sets it aside for the armorer. "That plate will be shaped into a command insignia or a shield boss. Worth more than the rest of the intake combined."

The remaining category one plates go to the armor workshop. Category two goes to the tool makers. Category three goes to the grinder.

The station master logs the intake. "Good eye on the grading. Most new sorters miss internal fractures and overgrade — send cracked plates to category one, where they fail under impact. Your categories are honest."

Honest grading saves lives downstream. A cracked plate in an armor set is a failure point in combat.

options.label: Complete the sorting and log the intake

steps.text:
The grinder is a stone mill — two discs of granite, one fixed, one rotating, with category three chitin fed through the gap. The output is a fine powder that gets mixed with binding agent to create composite panels.

You feed fragments into the hopper. The mill churns, crushing chitin between the stones, producing a pale amber powder that collects in a trough below. The sound is a constant, low-pitched grinding — the voice of recycling.

The powder is surprisingly uniform — the mill's stones are dressed with precision grooves that ensure consistent particle size. Too coarse and the composite is weak. Too fine and it won't bond properly. The station master checks the output with a mesh screen.

"Good grind. See how it flows through the forty-mesh but stops at sixty? That's the sweet spot." She scoops a handful and lets it cascade through her fingers. "This powder, mixed with mucus and pressed into molds, creates panels as strong as the original chitin. The hive wastes nothing."

You process twenty-three fragments into six pounds of powder. The powder goes to the composite workshop, where it'll be mixed, molded, and cured into wall panels, equipment cases, and emergency patches for armor.

The full cycle takes about a week — shed chitin to sorted grades to processed material to finished product. Every plate that falls from a Mandiblor's body becomes something useful. In the hive, even your own shed skin serves a purpose.

The station master seals the powder container. "Same amount tomorrow. The Mandiblors never stop shedding and we never stop collecting. That's the cycle."

options.label: Clean the mill and end the shift
